title: Frankincense and cedarwood cold-process soap
subtitle: A resinous, woody bar with a deep, meditative scent
excerpt: A cold-process bar scented with frankincense and cedarwood essential oils, built on a simple olive, coconut, and castor base. The resin-and-wood fragrance combination is one of the longer-lasting in soap; both essential oils fix well through the cure.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat; water 33% of oil weight. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997). Essential oil retention in cold-process soap: Robert Tisserand and Rodney Young, Essential Oil Safety (2nd ed., 2014), volatility data. Frankincense and cedarwood are among the most soap-stable essential oils.

recipe.batchNotes: Run any oil weight changes through a lye calculator. Essential oils scale proportionally.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 48 hours in the mould, then 4 to 6 weeks on a curing rack.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store cured bars on an open rack in a cool dark place. Fully cured bars keep two years. The cedarwood and frankincense fragrance holds better than citrus essential oils, often persisting through the full shelf life. Discard on signs of DOS or a sharp paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between fat and sodium hydroxide that produces soap and glycerine. The SAP value for each oil determines how much lye is needed.
The percentage of fat left unsaponified in the bar. At 5% superfat, the bar retains a small conditioning oil reserve and is mild on the skin.
The stage where soap batter thickens to a loose custard and a drizzle on the surface briefly holds its shape, indicating the lye and oils have emulsified.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Turn out the loaf after 2 days. Cut into bars wearing protective gloves. The cedarwood note is strong at cutting.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on the forearm. If there is no sting the bar is ready. Both oils fix well; the scent at 4 weeks is richer than at cutting.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: A fully cured bar is hard, cream-coloured, and carries the resinous-woody scent through use.

content.text: Frankincense and cedarwood are among the most stable essential oils in cold-process soap; both are heavy molecules that survive the alkaline environment and the heat of
saponification

far better than lighter citrus or herbal oils. The result is a bar that smells of resin and warm wood both when fresh-cut and through the full cure. The formula is a simple three-oil base: olive oil for a gentle, conditioning lather; coconut oil for cleansing strength and bubbly lather; castor oil to hold the foam. At 5%
superfat
, the bar is mild and suitable for most skin types. The high olive oil content means a longer cure is better; 6 weeks gives a noticeably harder bar than 4. Yield is 8 bars of around 115 g each.
Put on protective gloves and goggles before handling the lye.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: 30% of oil weight; melt gently if solid
NaOH at 99% purity; 5% superfat on this blend
2.5% of oil weight; resinous, warm top note
1.5% of oil weight; woody base note

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Work in a ventilated space with gloves and goggles on. Weigh the sodium hydroxide. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE lye pitcher. Add lye to water in a steady stream, stirring constantly. Set aside to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Prepare the oils
Melt the coconut oil in a heatproof bowl over low heat. Add the olive oil and castor oil. Stir to combine and cool to 38 to 42°C. Measure the essential oils into a small cup.
Combine, blend, and pour
Pour the lye into the oils in a slow stream. Use the stick blender in short bursts until the batter reaches light
trace
. Stir in the essential oils. Pour into the silicone loaf mould, tap once to settle, cover with cardboard, and wrap in a towel. Leave undisturbed for 48 hours.
Unmould, cut, and cure

Turn out the loaf after 2 days. A high-olive-oil bar may be slightly soft; if so, leave another day before cutting. Cut into bars wearing protective gloves and cure on a rack for 4 to 6 weeks in a cool dry space.
Storage and shelf life
Store cured bars on an open rack in a cool dark cupboard. These bars keep two years and the fragrance holds well through most of that time. Discard on signs of DOS or paint-like smell.
